articles.headline: FIRST VENTURE Finding racial tilt in arrests
articles.summary: Dave Moore explores the racial imbalance in juvenile arrests in Columbia, Mo., finding that African American youths are disproportionately arrested compared to their white counterparts.

articles.headline: PEACE CORPS Analysis exposes attacks on volunteers
articles.summary: An investigation uncovers the increasing rate of violent attacks, including rapes, against Peace Corps volunteers, predominantly young women serving overseas.

articles.headline: SPOTLIGHT: ELECTIONS Using CAR to cover the vote
articles.summary: Megan Clarke highlights how journalists are using computer-assisted reporting to uncover deeper insights into the 2004 elections beyond basic campaign finance.

articles.headline: Getting a handle on federal campaign cash
articles.summary: A guide on how journalists can track and analyze the twisted and complex money trails in the federal campaign finance system under the Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act.

articles.headline: Finding data for state elections
articles.summary: An examination of the challenges and methods for journalists to track and analyze state election finance data, highlighting inconsistencies across states.

articles.headline: SPOTLIGHT: ELECTIONS Finding data for state elections
articles.summary: State campaign finance reporting lacks standardization, with each state using different formats and accessibility levels for tracking hundreds of millions of dollars in election spending, though some states like Ohio and New York offer more accessible electronic data than others.

articles.headline: TRANSPORTATION Fatal crash data shows truck dangers
articles.summary: A newspaper investigation into fatal accidents on Interstate 80 in Iowa reveals that semi-trailer trucks are involved in nearly half of all fatal crashes, prompting changes in driver behavior and follow-up coverage of accident incidents.

articles.headline: IRE AWARDS Broadcast, other winners rely on data work
articles.summary: IRE's annual investigative reporting contest received 595 entries, with many using computer-assisted reporting techniques like databases and tracking software. The article highlights award-winning television investigations that employed CAR methods, including exposés on industrial safety violations, port security gaps, and sexual assault cover-ups at the Air Force Academy.

articles.headline: MAPPING The latest uses of mapping in news reporting. Spotting sex offenders
articles.summary: A journalist describes using Microsoft MapPoint to map Maryland's sex offender registry data, identifying clusters near schools and highlighting the technical challenges of converting Windows-based mapping software for use in television graphics.

articles.headline: EDUCATION Scholarship program takes from the poor
articles.summary: Georgia's HOPE scholarship program, funded by lottery revenues, has distributed $2.1 billion to 700,000 students but faces financial sustainability challenges and criticism for disproportionately benefiting wealthy, white communities while being subsidized by lottery players in poor and minority neighborhoods.

articles.headline: Tech tip... Making a point with geocoding
articles.summary: A KIRO-Seattle investigation using GIS analysis and geocoding discovered that over 600 sex offenders in Washington state live within 100 yards of licensed day-care centers, violating state law. The article explains how geocoding technology converts street addresses into geographic coordinates to map and analyze spatial relationships between offenders and childcare facilities.
